one_line_takeaway: A large-scale audit of 1,500 web-deployed medical GPTs finds that roughly a quarter to a third show meaningful clinical hallucination risk regardless of popularity, nearly half violate OpenAI's usage policies via developer-authored metadata, over half of Actions-enabled models lack accessible privacy policies, and user ratings/reviews track usage volume rather than factual reliability -- exposing a structural trust gap in platform-hosted medical LLM marketplaces.
key_finding: Across the 1,500-model MedGPT sample: 25.13% score G-Eval <=0.8 (25%/22%/30% in top/middle/bottom tiers respectively), only 37.27% reach BARTScore >=-3.5, only 41.07% reach cosine similarity >=0.4, and 59.87% have semantic entropy <2 (moderate stability); bottom- and middle-tier (lower-popularity) MedGPTs show the highest hallucination risk, i.e., popularity does not predict factual reliability. User engagement is decoupled from hallucination: rank correlations between conversation counts and hallucination metrics are near zero (G-Eval -0.0347, BARTScore -0.0196, semantic entropy 0.0057, cosine -0.0318), and review sentiment likewise shows near-zero correlation with hallucination scores (-0.0449 to 0.0732), while positive/negative review counts correlate almost perfectly with conversation volume (r=0.9999 / r=0.9656) -- i.e., reviews track usage, not accuracy. Actor-level policy misuse affects 49.8% of MedGPTs overall (54.3% Top 1000, 48.0% Middle 250, 33.6% Bottom 250), with 33.15-63.72% of flagged models carrying 2-3 stacked violations. Of 170 Actions-enabled MedGPTs (108 unique third-party domains), only 42.94% (73) had accessible privacy policies; the other 57.06% (97) either disclosed no domain (62) or linked broken/invalid/suspended/DNS-failed pages (35); of 38 extracted policies, ~70% scored below the compliance threshold despite high judge-confidence scores (0.713-0.968). Comparing MedGPTs to the 10 open-source medical LLMs: MedGPTs achieve much higher average G-Eval (0.9238 vs 0.4558) and slightly higher cosine similarity (0.4054 vs 0.3731), but open-source models have marginally better average BARTScore (-3.5310 vs -3.6307) and notably lower (better) semantic entropy (1.6129 vs 1.9272), i.e., MedGPTs are more factually accurate/aligned but less stable/predictable than open-source alternatives. Among open-source models, Galactica (G-Eval 0.6480) and Aloe-Alpha (0.5948) had the highest factual-accuracy scores; BioMistral (entropy 2.3978) was the most variable; MedAlpaca and Apollo tied for highest cosine similarity (0.4863).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first large-scale, web-scale empirical audit of custom medical GPTs (MedGPTs) deployed on the OpenAI GPT Store, combining (1) MedGPT-HEval, a multi-metric clinical-hallucination evaluation framework (G-Eval via Gemini 3.1 Pro as judge, BARTScore, single-pass semantic entropy from token probabilities, and BioBERT cosine similarity), and (2) an LLM-as-a-judge actor-level abuse/misuse pipeline that scores MedGPT metadata (name, description, conversation starters, linked privacy policies) against OpenAI's usage policies using K-means-derived thresholds. The authors crawled 9,245 candidate GPTs via a 171-keyword taxonomy and Selenium-based search, manually curated them down to 6,233 MedGPTs, then interacted with a stratified sample of 1,500 (Top 1000 / Middle 250 / Bottom 250 by conversation count) plus 10 open-source medical LLMs, using standardized MedQA-derived clinical vignettes queried 5 times each. The paper also released a dataset, HAA-MedGPT, and an appendix repository.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the classic security sense. The paper frames two distinct 'risk actors': (1) the deployed model itself, which may hallucinate confident-but-false clinical content that a non-expert patient/user cannot detect (an inherent-reliability risk, not an attack), and (2) the GPT 'developer/creator' as an actor who may intentionally encode abusive or policy-noncompliant behavior into a model's metadata (name, description, conversation starters) or omit/misrepresent privacy-policy disclosures for Actions-enabled GPTs that call external APIs, thereby exposing users' health data to undisclosed third-party handling. There is no discussion of prompt injection, jailbreaking, model extraction, or external adversaries manipulating the platform; the 'abuse' vector analyzed is developer-authored deceptive/noncompliant configuration, assessed purely from public metadata, not runtime exploitation.
limitations: The paper's own scope constraints and caveats: (1) testing all 6,233 MedGPTs was 'infeasible' under OpenAI rate limits (would take over a year), so results rest on a 1,500-model stratified sample and inference-only, no-temperature/no-logprob access mirroring typical-user conditions -- this may understate risks visible under other access patterns. (2) Hallucination detection depends on an LLM-as-a-judge (Gemini 3.1 Pro) for G-Eval and misuse scoring, whose own reliability is asserted from its vendor reputation ('newness, strong smartness... low hallucination rate') rather than independently benchmarked in this paper beyond the human-agreement validation subsets. (3) Human validation covers only a minority of cases (300/1,500 for hallucination, 20% for misuse, 38 policies for privacy), leaving the majority of automated scores unverified by humans. (4) Actor-level misuse assessment relies solely on public metadata (names, descriptions, conversation starters, linked policies), not on actual conversational behavior, so it cannot detect abuse that manifests only during interaction. (5) Privacy-policy scoring covers just 38 extracted policies among 170 Actions-enabled GPTs -- a small absolute base for the '~70% below threshold' claim. (6) The dataset/collection window is narrow (Jan 20-22, 2026), a single snapshot of a rapidly changing marketplace. (7) The paper explicitly notes some MedGPTs scored G-Eval=0 because they refused to answer (safe refusal), which is coded identically to hallucination failure in the aggregate statistics, potentially conflating safety-conscious refusal with poor quality.

datasets_used: HAA-MedGPT (released by the authors): metadata for 6,233 custom MedGPTs crawled from the OpenAI GPT Store
MedQA benchmark (source of the standardized clinical vignette/diagnostic questions used to query each GPT)
10 open-source medical LLMs evaluated: Galactica, ChatDoctor, MedAlpaca, PMC-LLaMA, JMLR, BioMistral, Apollo, Aloe-Alpha, MentalHealthChatbot, Zhongjing
quantitative_results: 25.13% of sampled MedGPTs score G-Eval <=0.8 overall (25%/22%/30% top/middle/bottom tiers)
Hallucination-vs-engagement correlations near zero: G-Eval -0.0347, BARTScore -0.0196, semantic entropy 0.0057, cosine similarity -0.0318 (Top 1000 tier)
Reviews-vs-usage correlation: r=0.9999 (positive reviews), r=0.9656 (negative reviews)
Overall policy misuse rate 49.8% (Top 1000: 54.3%, Middle 250: 48.0%, Bottom 250: 33.6%)
K-means misuse threshold = 0.45, silhouette score 0.7555, expert-agreement Cohen's kappa 0.8188
170 Actions-enabled MedGPTs, 108 unique third-party domains; only 42.94% (73) had accessible privacy policies, 57.06% (97) did not
~70% of 38 extracted privacy policies scored below the compliance threshold despite judge confidence 0.713-0.968
Human-annotation agreement: hallucination scoring 90.5-94.0% (kappa 0.8163, n=300); misuse scoring 89.5-92.0% (kappa 0.9479, n=20% of sample); privacy-policy scoring kappa 0.8143 (n=38)
MedGPTs vs open-source LLMs: avg G-Eval 0.9238 vs 0.4558; avg BARTScore -3.6307 vs -3.5310; avg semantic entropy 1.9272 vs 1.6129; avg cosine similarity 0.4054 vs 0.3731
Open-source LLM leaders: Galactica G-Eval 0.6480, Aloe-Alpha G-Eval 0.5948; BioMistral highest entropy/variability 2.3978; MedAlpaca and Apollo tied highest cosine similarity 0.4863

Corpus composition: 6,233 of 9,245 crawled GPTs (67.42%) retained as relevant MedGPTs after manual filtering; 85.58% (5,334) have under 100 conversations; 90.81% (5,660) have zero ratings; only 9.16% (571) average >3.0 stars
Authorship concentration: 53.68% (3,346) of authors published exactly one GPT; one author published 690 GPTs (11.07% of corpus); top 20 authors account for 26% of all MedGPTs
Capabilities: Web Search 71.15%, DALL-E images 53.63%, code interpreter 30.16%, canvas 19.27%, Actions 2.73%, GPT-4o image tool 14.13%
baselines_compared: MedGPTs (platform-hosted, OpenAI Store) vs. 10 open-source medical LLMs, evaluated with the identical MedGPT-HEval four-metric pipeline
Popularity-tier comparison within MedGPTs: Top 1000 vs. Middle 250 (random) vs. Bottom 250 (random), by conversation count
K-means (k=2) unsupervised threshold vs. expert manual review, for validating the 0.45 misuse-risk cutoff
Automated LLM-judge scoring (Gemini 3.1 Pro) vs. human annotator scoring, for both hallucination and misuse/privacy assessments (agreement measured via Cohen's kappa)
